bins.name: 200 MW Data Center Threshold
bins.desc: FirstEnergy argues that any rule should cover only data center customers at 200 MW or above. Including all loads at 20 MW risks clogging the queue, imposing burdens on non-data-center industrial customers, and undermining manufacturing and job-creation goals.
quotes: if the Commission elects to promulgate a rule for the interconnection of large load data center interconnections, it should do so in a targeted manner.
the Commission should limit any rule to (1) data centers that (2) have loads of at least 200 MW.
Sweeping in all large loads of 20 MW and above risks clogging interconnection queues, creating long interconnection wait times, and threatening the expedient interconnection of new data center load.

Imposing a 20 MW large load interconnection threshold also may result in prohibitory costs and onerous regulatory hurdles for other types of load customers, harming economic development that relies on the efficient and cost-effective interconnection of manufacturers and other traditional large load customers.

bins.name: Milestone-Based Expedited Queue
bins.desc: FirstEnergy supports an expedited interconnection track for projects meeting technical, financial, and permitting milestones, consistent with PJM's proposed framework and Order No. 2023 clustering principles. This would clear speculative projects and reduce backlogs.
quotes: The Commission should consider reforms in this proceeding that allow interconnection-ready projects to timely advance through the queue, similar to PJM Interconnection, L.L.C.'s ("PJM") consideration of rules for an expedited interconnection track for the interconnection of data centers and other large loads.

By allowing projects that meet defined technical, financial, and permitting milestones to proceed ahead of other less-developed requests, transmission providers can reduce unnecessary delays and lower the queue backlog.
Implementation and administration of an option to build for large load data center customers will add significant delay and risk to their proposed interconnections.

bins.name: Oppose Direct Cost Assignment
bins.desc: FirstEnergy opposes the ANOPR's proposal to directly assign 100% of network upgrade costs to load customers. It argues this raises jurisdictional problems between FERC and state retail regulators and would cause years of litigation, slowing rather than accelerating interconnections.
quotes: the ANOPR's suggestion that "load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through the interconnection studies" threatens to inhibit this goal.

The proposed direct assignment of network upgrade costs to an end-use customer raises thorny jurisdictional issues that will result in years of delay, thereby frustrating efficient and timely data center interconnections.

Rather than risk extended delay due to jurisdictional challenges and other cost allocation battles, the Commission should employ its long-standing rolled-in rate approach for network upgrades in conjunction with facilitating customer protections to ensure that other retail load customers are not adversely affected by large load interconnections.

the Commission should use its traditional rolled-in rate approach to ensure that wholesale transmission customers pay their fair share of network upgrade costs that benefit all customers, and the Commission should allow state regulators and load-serving entities to use tariff and/or agreement provisions at the retail level to ensure that large load customers pay their fair share of retail-level costs.

bins.name: Hybrid Facility Cost Concern
bins.desc: FirstEnergy flags that the ANOPR's direct-assignment proposal covers hybrid facilities, which raises the same jurisdictional cost-allocation concerns as for pure load customers.
quotes: the ANOPR's suggestion that "load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through the interconnection studies" threatens to inhibit this goal.

bins.name: Retail Customer Protections
bins.desc: FirstEnergy supports protecting existing retail customers from cost shifts driven by large load interconnections, but argues this can be achieved through rolled-in rates combined with state tariff and agreement mechanisms rather than direct assignment.
quotes: Rather than risk extended delay due to jurisdictional challenges and other cost allocation battles, the Commission should employ its long-standing rolled-in rate approach for network upgrades in conjunction with facilitating customer protections to ensure that other retail load customers are not adversely affected by large load interconnections.

bins.name: Queue Study Reforms
bins.desc: FirstEnergy endorses milestone-based, cluster-style interconnection study reforms that let viable projects advance ahead of speculative ones, drawing on lessons from generator interconnection processes and PJM's proposed large-load track.
quotes: The Commission should consider reforms in this proceeding that allow interconnection-ready projects to timely advance through the queue, similar to PJM Interconnection, L.L.C.'s ("PJM") consideration of rules for an expedited interconnection track for the interconnection of data centers and other large loads.

By allowing projects that meet defined technical, financial, and permitting milestones to proceed ahead of other less-developed requests, transmission providers can reduce unnecessary delays and lower the queue backlog.

bins.name: Rolled-In Rate Cost Allocation
bins.desc: FirstEnergy supports the Commission's long-standing policy of rolling network upgrade costs into transmission rates, arguing this avoids jurisdictional disputes and is already paired with utility-level customer protections. It also contends the option to build has not delivered savings even for generators.
quotes: the ANOPR's suggestion that "load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned through the interconnection studies" threatens to inhibit this goal.

The proposed direct assignment of network upgrade costs to an end-use customer raises thorny jurisdictional issues that will result in years of delay, thereby frustrating efficient and timely data center interconnections.

Rather than risk extended delay due to jurisdictional challenges and other cost allocation battles, the Commission should employ its long-standing rolled-in rate approach for network upgrades in conjunction with facilitating customer protections to ensure that other retail load customers are not adversely affected by large load interconnections.

the Commission should use its traditional rolled-in rate approach to ensure that wholesale transmission customers pay their fair share of network upgrade costs that benefit all customers, and the Commission should allow state regulators and load-serving entities to use tariff and/or agreement provisions at the retail level to ensure that large load customers pay their fair share of retail-level costs.

FirstEnergy views the option to build for generator interconnection customers as a failed tariff mechanism that has not resulted in meaningful value or savings that were expected by independent power producers.

bins.name: Utilities Should Build Upgrades
bins.desc: FirstEnergy argues that experienced transmission-owning utilities are better positioned than data center customers to design, permit, and build interconnection facilities, and that keeping construction with utilities is the fastest path to power.
quotes: the best way to achieve the ANOPR's goal of timely large load interconnections is to have experienced transmission-owning utilities design and build the transmission facilities needed to interconnect large load customers.

bins.name: PJM Context and Precedent
bins.desc: FirstEnergy operates in PJM and cites PJM's proposed expedited large-load interconnection track as a model for Commission reform. Its threshold and queue recommendations align with PJM-specific conditions.
quotes: the Commission should limit any rule to (1) data centers that (2) have loads of at least 200 MW.

The Commission should consider reforms in this proceeding that allow interconnection-ready projects to timely advance through the queue, similar to PJM Interconnection, L.L.C.'s ("PJM") consideration of rules for an expedited interconnection track for the interconnection of data centers and other large loads.

bins.name: Harm to Non-Data-Center Customers
bins.desc: FirstEnergy warns that a 20 MW threshold sweeps in manufacturers and other industrial customers, imposing prohibitory costs and regulatory burdens on customers who are not the intended target of the rule, and undermining reshoring and job-creation goals.
quotes: Imposing a 20 MW large load interconnection threshold also may result in prohibitory costs and onerous regulatory hurdles for other types of load customers, harming economic development that relies on the efficient and cost-effective interconnection of manufacturers and other traditional large load customers.

bins.name: FERC vs State Retail Jurisdiction
bins.desc: FirstEnergy identifies a core jurisdictional problem: data center customers are retail customers regulated by states, not FERC. Direct cost assignment by FERC would intrude on state retail rate authority and invite years of appellate challenge. The solution is wholesale rolled-in rates paired with state-level mechanisms.
quotes: The proposed direct assignment of network upgrade costs to an end-use customer raises thorny jurisdictional issues that will result in years of delay, thereby frustrating efficient and timely data center interconnections.

the Commission should use its traditional rolled-in rate approach to ensure that wholesale transmission customers pay their fair share of network upgrade costs that benefit all customers, and the Commission should allow state regulators and load-serving entities to use tariff and/or agreement provisions at the retail level to ensure that large load customers pay their fair share of retail-level costs.

bins.name: Option to Build Should Be Rejected
bins.desc: FirstEnergy opposes extending the option to build to load customers, calling it a failed mechanism even for generators. Data center customers lack NERC compliance obligations, permitting expertise, and utility-standard construction capability, leaving transmission owners liable for facilities they did not build.
quotes: FirstEnergy views the option to build for generator interconnection customers as a failed tariff mechanism that has not resulted in meaningful value or savings that were expected by independent power producers.
Implementation and administration of an option to build for large load data center customers will add significant delay and risk to their proposed interconnections.
As end-use customers, large load customers have no obligations to protect or maintain the reliability of the transmission system, including compliance with applicable NERC standards.
Providing load customers an option to build will put the transmission owner in an untenable position whereby it has compliance responsibilities for facilities that it did not engineer or build.

the best way to achieve the ANOPR's goal of timely large load interconnections is to have experienced transmission-owning utilities design and build the transmission facilities needed to interconnect large load customers.

bins.name: Reliability Obligations Gap
bins.desc: Load customers are not subject to NERC reliability standards and lack the compliance obligations needed to safely build and operate interconnection facilities. Transmission owners would bear NERC responsibility for customer-built facilities, an untenable arrangement.
quotes: As end-use customers, large load customers have no obligations to protect or maintain the reliability of the transmission system, including compliance with applicable NERC standards.
Providing load customers an option to build will put the transmission owner in an untenable position whereby it has compliance responsibilities for facilities that it did not engineer or build.
